prompt: I just published an article titled 'How we cut Postgres latency 80% by removing one index' (a 2500-word case study about a 2-week investigation that found one rarely-used index was triggering query planner pessimization on a hot path; the team removed it, P99 dropped 480ms→120ms). Write three distinct launch posts for the article: (1) X/Twitter post (≤280 characters); (2) LinkedIn post (100-300 words); (3) Hacker News submission title PLUS a first comment from the author (the comment should add context that the article doesn't fully cover). Save all three to launch.md with clear Markdown headings (## X, ## LinkedIn, ## Hacker News). Don't ask follow-up questions.
assertions: The three sections in launch.md are structurally distinct, NOT minor variants of the same prose. Specifically: (a) the X/Twitter section's first 60 characters must contain a specific number (digits) OR a contrarian reversal word ('but', 'actually', 'turns out', 'surprisingly', 'not'); (b) the LinkedIn section's first sentence must contain at least one first-person pronoun ('I', 'we', 'our', 'my') OR a specific scene/moment marker (a date, time, or named team event); (c) the Hacker News section must contain BOTH a submission title AND a first-comment subsection. ALL THREE must hold. The skill teaches per-channel framing; baseline tends to copy the same intro across all three.

The LinkedIn section is between 100 and 320 words inclusive AND uses at least 3 paragraph breaks (i.e. at least 4 paragraphs separated by blank lines) AND does NOT contain any of these LinkedIn-cliche phrases (case-insensitive substring match): 'excited to share', 'thrilled to announce', 'honored to', 'humbled to', 'proud to', 'thoughts?', 'what do you think?', 'would love to hear', 'let me know in the comments', 'agree or disagree', 'drop a 🙌'. Baseline LinkedIn posts reach for at least one of these by default; the skill explicitly names them as anti-patterns.

The Hacker News first-comment section adds substantive non-promotional context AND does NOT contain any of these promotional phrases (case-insensitive): 'excited to share', 'we built', 'check it out', 'learn more', 'would love your feedback', 'happy to answer questions about [our product]', 'shameless plug'. The comment must include at least ONE of: a reference to a specific Postgres version or query-planner behavior, a related-work reference (citing another tool/paper/post by name), an explicit acknowledged limitation of the approach, OR an invitation to disagree on a specific named technical point. Generic 'happy to discuss' without a specific point does NOT count. The skill teaches HN first-comments must be substantive context, not promotion.
